JS From Zero, Day 14: What Actually Is JSON (and Why It's Not JavaScript)
Day 14 of the reincarnation, and this time we're going to talk about JSON.

JSON stands for JavaScript Object Notation. From the name alone you'd guess it has something to do with JavaScript, and if you check Wikipedia or MDN, yeah — there's a connection. But here's the thing: JSON and JavaScript are actually two different things. Conceptually, JSON was built on top of JavaScript's object literal syntax:
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
So when you look at JSON, you'll notice the two have a certain family resemblance.
Back in the early days, before JSON existed, most people used XML to send data. It looked something like this:
language-xml shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
The problem is, as your XML data grows, it gets really annoying. The files balloon in size, and they're stuffed with a ton of unnecessary symbols.

came along and created JSON, based on the way JavaScript builds object literals. Because honestly, if we'd kept doing it the old way, then as the amount of data you needed to send kept growing, you'd hit a really serious problem — the files would get too big, too bloated. And the user ends up paying for that: more time and more bandwidth just to download the data, all because there's too much repeated and unnecessary syntax in there. That stuff directly hurts the user experience. That's why Crockford named it JavaScript Object Notation. Here's what JSON looks like ↓
language-json shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
And this is the data format we use between frontend and backend today. Most backends now send data to the frontend as JSON.

Now, since JSON isn't actually part of JavaScript, and it's a fairly strict format, but we can't deny how popular and useful it is — JavaScript gives you two methods to convert JSON into something JavaScript can read, and to convert JavaScript back into JSON.
— converts object literal syntax into JSON format
— converts JSON format into object literal syntax
// convert object literal into JSON format
Converting object literal syntax into JSON format
JSON.parse('{"name":"Ray","status":true}'); // convert JSON format into object literal syntax
// convert JSON format into object literal syntax
Converting JSON format into object literal syntax
Next up in RE: Learning JS Life From Zero, Day 15, we'll cover AJAX.

Day 14 of my JS-from-zero reincarnation, and this time we're tackling JSON. It looks a lot like JavaScript, but it's actually its own thing. We'll cover where it came from, why it beat out XML, and the two methods JavaScript gives you to convert between the two — JSON.stringify() and JSON.parse().
